retrieved_docs.title: Gastrointestinal Neuroendocrine Tumors -- Evaluation -- Imaging Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Somatostatin receptor imaging Somatostatin receptor scintigraphy (eg, Octreoscan) and positron emission tomography (PET) imaging with DOTATOC (1,4,7,10-tetraazacyclododecane-1,4,7,10-tetraacetic acid Tyr³-octreotide) or similar radiolabeled somatostatin analogs are highly effective for identifying somatostatin receptor-expressing tumors and evaluating the extent of disease. Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Octreotide Scan ," for further information. In NECs, these modalities may have limited utility due to poor differentiation and variable receptor expression. [18] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Glucagonoma -- Evaluation -- Imaging Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagonomas express abundant somatostatin receptors as compared to other pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. Hence, they are suitable for localization using somatostatin analogues, eg, octreotide. Somatostatin receptor scintigraphy (SRS) was positive in 97% of patients with glucagonoma, as noted in a study by Kindmark et al. [12] SRS also has the advantage of detecting small, occult metastases within and outside the abdomen, and is therefore helpful in staging the tumor.

retrieved_docs.title: Gastric Neuroendocrine Tumors -- Evaluation -- Imaging Studies
retrieved_docs.content: For localized disease, EUS is valuable in assessing tumor invasion depth and lymph node involvement. Type 1 and type 2 GNETs are often confined to the mucosa and submucosa, whereas type 3 GNETs may show deeper infiltration. For systemic staging, contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CT) or magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the abdomen and pelvis is recommended, particularly for type 3 GNETs or those with high-risk features (size >2 cm, Ki-67 >2%, or invasion beyond the submucosa). Functional imaging with somatostatin receptor-based modalities, such as 68 Ga-dotatate positron emission tomography/computed tomography (PET/CT) or octreotide scintigraphy (Octreoscan), helps detect metastases and assess somatostatin receptor expression, which has therapeutic implications for peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (PRRT). In poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinomas, 18 F-fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) PET/CT may be more helpful, as these tumors often have high metabolic activity. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Neuroendocrine Tumors -- Medical Oncology -- Peptide Receptor Radionuclide Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: PRRT with 177 Lu-DOTATATE offers targeted radiotherapy for patients with somatostatin receptor-positive, well-differentiated pNETs. This therapy delivers radiation directly to tumor cells, leading to tumor regression and symptom relief. Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Neuroendocrine Tumor Lu 177 Dotate Therapy ," for further information on 177 Lu-dotatate therapy. [42] The NETTER-1 trial demonstrated a significant improvement in progression-free survival and quality of life with 177Lu-dotatate compared to high-dose octreotide LAR in patients with metastatic midgut NETs. The NETTER-2 trial established extended progression-free survival with 177 Lu-dotatate and high-dose octreotide LAR in patients with grade 2 or 3 advanced gastroenteropancreatic NETs, supporting its potential as first-line therapy for more aggressive NETs. While generally well-tolerated, potential hematologic and renal toxicities necessitate careful patient selection and monitoring. [37] [43] [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Nuclear Medicine PET/CT Gastrointestinal Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanism by which 68Ga-DOTANOC PET/CT targets SSTRs expressed by well-differentiated NETs is similarly utilized in delivering personalized molecular radiotherapy (MRT) to these tumors, a procedure known as peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (PRRT). In PRRT, gallium-68 is replaced with a therapeutic radiotracer, most commonly lutetium-177. Prior to initiating therapy, disease mapping through gallium PET or octreotide imaging determines patient suitability. Effective therapy requires that most tumor sites demonstrate radiotracer uptake above that of the liver. Following therapy, planar imaging confirms adequate radiotherapy distribution within the tumor sites. [42] This palliative therapy is being extensively studied to evaluate survival benefits in selected patient populations, offering a promising option for managing advanced neuroendocrine tumors.

retrieved_docs.title: Somatostatinoma -- Evaluation -- Imaging Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Functional imaging (PET): Somatostatin receptor-based positron emission tomography (PET) using radiolabeled ligands such as 68 Ga-DOTATATE, 68 Ga-DOTATOC, 68 Ga-DOTANOC, or 64 Cu-DOTATATE demonstrates excellent sensitivity and specificity for tumor localization and staging. High radiotracer uptake also serves as a predictive biomarker for responsiveness to PRRT.

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Neuroendocrine Tumors -- Evaluation -- 68 Ga-DOTATATE imaging
retrieved_docs.content: 68 Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT has emerged as the gold standard in the functional imaging of well-differentiated pNETs. This modality targets somatostatin receptor subtype 2, which is overexpressed in most well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumors. It allows for whole-body imaging and is particularly effective in detecting small primary tumors, lymphatic spread, and distant metastases. The sensitivity of Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT is approximately 90% to 95%, with specificity ranging from 88% to 95%. Please refer to StatPearls' companion resource, " Octreotide Scan ," for additional information. [23] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: APUDoma(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The tumor extent and location can be determined using imaging, such as computed tomography (CT) or magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans using new techniques and contrast media that increase sensitivity and specificity of detecting various NETs. [13] Because GEP-NETs express somatostatin receptors (SSTRs) on their surfaces, somatostatin receptor scintigraphy (SRS) using radiolabeled octreotide, a somatostatin mimic, to bind somatostatin receptors thus allowing tumor detection, localization, and the existence of metastasis. The exception to this is insulinoma, which has a low level of somatostatin receptor expression. [16] Positron emission tomography (PET) scan with gallium-DOTATOC, if available, could replace SSR due to its higher sensitivity. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Neuroendocrine Tumors -- Radiation Oncology -- Peptide Receptor Radionuclide Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (PRRT) has become a validated systemic treatment strategy for patients with advanced, unresectable, or metastatic well-differentiated pNETs that express somatostatin receptors. PRRT utilizes radiolabeled somatostatin analogs, such as ^177Lu-DOTATATE, to deliver targeted internal radiation, resulting in tumor cytotoxicity and clinical benefit. This modality is most effective in tumors with a Ki-67 index of ≤20% and strong somatostatin receptor expression, as confirmed by Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT. PRRT is associated with high disease control rates, meaningful symptomatic improvement, and a generally favorable toxicity profile, making it a key component of treatment for receptor-positive pNETs. [37] [39] [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Octreotide Scan -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Tumors such as pituitary adenomas, gastrointestinal tract NETs, lung NETs, pheochromocytomas, and paragangliomas typically show predominant expression of SSTR2. In contrast, adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH)-secreting pituitary adenomas (SSTR5), insulinomas (SSTR1 and SSTR5), and medullary thyroid carcinomas predominantly express other SSTR subtypes. Both normal and tumor cells can exhibit varying patterns of SSTR subtype expression. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Somatostatinoma -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The evaluation of somatostatinoma requires a combination of clinical suspicion, biochemical testing, imaging studies, and histopathologic confirmation. Localization and staging rely on cross-sectional imaging studies, eg, computed tomography (CT), magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), endoscopic ultrasound (EUS), and somatostatin receptor-based positron emission tomography (PET) scans. Surgical resection is the primary treatment for localized disease, while unresectable or metastatic cases are managed with somatostatin analogs, peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (PRRT), and targeted therapies. Prognosis depends on tumor grade, stage, and resectability, with better outcomes in localized, well-differentiated tumors.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroendocrine Tumor 177Lutetium-Dotatate Therapy -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: An interprofessional team provides an integrated approach to treatment decisions, aiming to achieve the best possible clinical outcomes. Clinicians in interventional radiology, medical oncology, and surgical oncology are critical stakeholders in individualized, patient-centered treatment decisions. Randomized clinical trials with a head-to-head comparison of other treatment options, eg, targeted therapies, are still being conducted; therefore, evidence on the optimal sequence of metastatic neuroendocrine tumor treatment is unavailable. Eligible patients are encouraged to enroll in open clinical trials.

retrieved_docs.title: Octreotide Scan -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: SSTRs are expressed in approximately 80% of gastrinomas. Pentetreotide scanning can successfully localize these neoplasms (whether primary or nodal metastases) in up to 78% to 86% of cases. [45] [46] [47] In a study involving 80 patients, Gibril et al reported that CT, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), or angiography identified extrahepatic gastrinomas in 28% to 31% of cases, whereas ultrasound detected extrahepatic tumors in only 9% of patients. [48] A pentetreotide scan localizes primary lesions and is valuable in screening for metastases and monitoring therapeutic response. [48] Currently, the use of hybrid SSTR PET/MRI for evaluating head and neck tumors is also being explored. [49] Other applications include SRS for monitoring appendicular NETs post-surgery, particularly when recurrence or metastasis is suspected. [50] In cases of progressive prostate carcinoma, which may undergo neuroendocrine transformation, SRS can help localize targets with neuroendocrine differentiation. [51]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroendocrine Tumor 177Lutetium-Dotatate Therapy -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: This treatment also has an impact on the quality of life. A study concluded that the time to deterioration in the quality of life was significantly longer with 177 Lu-dotatate compared with octreotide in several domains, including global health status, physical functioning, role functioning, fatigue, pain, diarrhea, disease-related worries, and body image. The differences were clinically significant in global health status, with median times to deterioration in quality of life of 28.8 months versus 6.1 months and physical functioning of 25.2 months versus 11.5 months. [20] In a later analysis, 5 years after the last patient, long-term outcomes were randomized with a median follow-up of 76 months in both groups. [21] [ 55 ] Notably, 12% of the 177 patients in the 177 Lu-dotatate group received further PRRT, whereas 36% of patients in the control group had a crossover to 177 Lu-dotatate. The final overall survival in the intent-to-treat population was not significantly different between the study groups (HR, 0.84; 95% CI, 0.60-1.17), likely due to the crossover design.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroendocrine Tumor 177Lutetium-Dotatate Therapy -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with symptomatic disease are often treated initially with somatostatin analogs that are highly effective in controlling the symptoms of carcinoid syndrome, but also control tumor growth. Treatment decisions are based on the patient's clinical presentation, including hormonal symptoms, and the type of treatment received before disease progression. NET patients need staging evaluation with Gallium-68 ( 68 Ga) dotatate or Indium 111 pentetreotide imaging to assess the expression of somatostatin receptors. Recently, 68 Ga-dotatate imaging has supplanted Indium 111 pentetreotide (ie, Octreoscan) imaging based on the availability of PET imaging. Copper 64 (Cu 64 ) is a PET isotope similar to 68 Ga that can be produced in quantities to meet the clinical needs of imaging centers without the supply shortages of nuclear-generator-based positron emission tomography (PET) isotopes. Cu 64 -dotatate has advantages over 68 Ga-dotatate in detecting significantly more NET lesions than 68 Ga-dotatate. The shelf life of Cu 64 is over 24 hours, and the scanning window of at least 3 hours makes Cu 64 -dotatate logistically more favorable to use in the outpatient clinical setting. [7] A subset of patients with clinical or radiological progression after somatostatin analogs are managed with debulking palliative surgery, liver-directed interventions (eg, chemoembolization), or systemic radiolabeled somatostatin analogs, eg, 177 Lu-dotatate.[ 7 ] [8] Most clinical studies reporting efficacy with radiolabeled somatostatin analogs have included PNETs and GINETs. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Gastrointestinal Neuroendocrine Tumors -- Evaluation -- Imaging Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Along with biochemical testing, a GI-NET diagnosis relies on imaging modalities, including computed tomography (CT), magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and somatostatin receptor-based imaging.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2198
retrieved_docs.content: Indium In-111 pentetreotide is a radiolabeled somatostatin analog that specifically targets somatostatin receptors, making it useful for diagnostic imaging of tissues that express these receptors, such as certain neuroendocrine tumors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 55
retrieved_docs.content: Technetium Tc 99m depreotide is a radiopharmaceutical that is mapped from the peptide hormone somatostatin, meaning it is a somatostatin analog labeled with technetium‑99m. By retaining the binding affinity of somatostatin for its receptors, technetium Tc 99m depreotide is used in nuclear medicine to image tissues that express somatostatin receptors, such as neuroendocrine tumors and other receptor‑positive lesions, thereby aiding diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 625
retrieved_docs.content: Indium-111 pentetreotide is a member of the Indium-111 tumour detection radiopharmaceuticals class and it specifically targets somatostatin receptors (receptory somatostatin). This targeting property makes Indium-111 pentetreotide useful for imaging tumors that express somatostatin receptors, such as many neuroendocrine neoplasms, thereby aiding in diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 1136
retrieved_docs.content: Indium-111 tumour detection radiopharmaceuticals are a subset of tumour detection radiopharmaceuticals. Within this class, indium In-111 pentetreotide is a member that specifically targets the receptory somatostatinu. By binding to the somatostatin receptor, indium In-111 pentetreotide enables nuclear imaging of tumors expressing this receptor, supporting diagnosis and therapeutic planning in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 288
retrieved_docs.content: Indium 111In‑satumomab pendetide is classified as a member of the Indium‑111 tumour detection radiopharmaceuticals group, a class of radiolabelled agents used for imaging malignant lesions. This radiopharmaceutical class also includes indium In‑111 pentetreotide, which specifically binds to somatostatin receptors (receptory somatostatin‑u) expressed on certain neuroendocrine tumors. By targeting these receptors, indium In‑111 pentetreotide enables precise visualization of receptor‑positive tumors, and the inclusion of Indium 111In‑satumomab pendetide within the same group underscores its role in tumor detection using Indium‑111–based imaging.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 729
retrieved_docs.content: Technetium‑99m compounds used for tumor detection constitute a class of tumour‑detection radiopharmaceuticals, and within this class the specific agent technetium Tc‑99m depreotide is included. Technetium Tc‑99m depreotide is a somatostatin‑derived radiopharmaceutical, meaning it is chemically mapped from the peptide hormone somatostatin and retains its ability to bind somatostatin receptors, which facilitates imaging of somatostatin‑expressing tumors. This relationship highlights that technetium‑99m tumour‑detection agents, exemplified by technetium Tc‑99m depreotide, are designed to target specific molecular markers (such as somatostatin receptors) to improve the accuracy of tumor imaging in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1349
retrieved_docs.content: Technetium Tc 99m depreotide is a technetium‑99m labeled compound that belongs to the class of Technetium 99m compounds used as tumor detection radiopharmaceuticals, and it is derived from the peptide hormone somatostatin, functioning as a somatostatin analog for imaging somatostatin‑receptor–expressing tissues.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 0 -- Community 9
retrieved_docs.content: agent used for imaging receptor‑expressing tumors. Additional diagnostic radiopharmaceuticals for tumor detection include fluorocholine, technetium‑99m arcitumomab and technetium‑99m depreotide (a somatostatin analogue), all classified within “Other diagnostic radiopharmaceuticals for tumour detection” and the broader “tumour detection radiopharmaceuticals” subgroup. Collectively, these entities delineate a hierarchical organization of antiviral, antifungal and diagnostic agents, their chemical and mechanistic linkages, therapeutic indications, fixed‑dose combinations, and clinically relevant contraindications, thereby informing both infectious‑disease management and oncologic imaging strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 321
retrieved_docs.content: Indium 111In‑satumomab pendetide and indium In‑111 pentetreotide are radiopharmaceutical agents that belong to the class of Indium‑111 tumour detection radiopharmaceuticals, which in turn are a specific type of tumour detection radiopharmaceuticals. Both agents are used for imaging malignant lesions by exploiting the radioactive properties of indium‑111. Indium In‑111 pentetreotide has a particular affinity for somatostatin receptors (receptory somatostatin u), allowing it to target and visualize tumours that express these receptors. This classification and targeting information together clarify the role of these indium‑111‑based compounds in diagnostic nuclear medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 0 -- Community 3
retrieved_docs.content: targeting somatostatin receptors for neuroendocrine‑tumour imaging), fluorocholine (a fluorine‑containing imaging agent), technetium‑99m arcitumomab (derived from a monoclonal antibody), and CP 609754 (a technetium‑99m derivative of arcitumomab). The monoclonal antibody tanezumab belongs to the broader class of MONOKLONÁLNÍ PROTILÁTKY, while abciximab is a monoclonal antibody used to inhibit platelet aggregation. PEPTIDE‑DERIVED compounds traced to oligopeptide scaffolds—acetylphenylalanyl‑prolyl‑boroarginine, deltorphin I Ala(2)‑, and the antiviral telaprevir—illustrate the therapeutic and diagnostic utility of short peptide sequences. Collectively, these entities are organized by chemical class, mechanism of action, and clinical indication, providing a comprehensive framework for targeted patient care across viral, bacterial, fungal, oncologic and gastro‑intestinal domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 38
retrieved_docs.content: includes the growth‑hormone analogues mecasermin, sermorelin, somapacitan, and tesamorelin; sermorelin’s presence in both the therapeutic and pituitary‑function‑test groups highlights the overlap between diagnostic and therapeutic categories. The thyrotropin class in ATC contains thyreotropin‑alpha, a thyroid‑stimulating hormone analogue. Together, these hierarchical classifications delineate the relationships among anterior pituitary hormone analogues, a wide array of diagnostic agents, and their clinical applications in endocrine evaluation, imaging, functional testing, and disease detection, guiding clinicians in the selection of appropriate agents for patient assessment and therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 673
retrieved_docs.content: Somatorelin and Metyrapon are both classified as pituitary function test diagnostic agents. As members of this group, they are used to evaluate the activity of the pituitary gland, helping clinicians assess hormone secretion and diagnose endocrine disorders that involve pituitary dysfunction. Their inclusion in the category of pituitary function test diagnostic agents highlights their specific role in testing and monitoring pituitary performance in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 61
retrieved_docs.content: is explicitly classified as a monoclonal antibody. Ibalizumab is mapped to the monoclonal‑antibody category, confirming its identity as a monoclonal antibody. Overall, the knowledge network links specific radiopharmaceutical agents (e.g., technetium Tc 99m arcitumomab, technetium Tc 99m depreotide, indium In‑111 pentetreotide) to their molecular origins (monoclonal antibodies, organotechnetium compounds, somatostatin, oligopeptides) and to their functional roles in tumor detection through receptor targeting or chemical composition. This integrated understanding aids clinicians and researchers in selecting appropriate imaging agents based on target biology and chemical class, thereby optimizing diagnostic accuracy and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 100
retrieved_docs.content: within the ATC tumor‑detection group, underscoring the importance of fluorine chemistry in oncologic imaging. Technetium‑99m‑based tumor agents are further specialized. Technetium‑99m‑depreotide is derived from a somatostatin analogue, retaining affinity for somatostatin receptors and enhancing imaging of neuroendocrine and other somatostatin‑receptor‑positive tumors. Technetium‑99m aggregated albumin represents an additional child agent within the technetium‑99m family. Collectively, these entities form an interconnected network: radiofarmaka encompass diagnostic radioisotopes; diagnostic radioisotopes and fluorine‑based agents such as fluorocholine and fluoride supplements serve specific imaging or preventive functions; and technetium‑99m agents engineered from biologically active peptides (e.g., somatostatin) improve tumor detection. Understanding these hierarchical and functional relationships aids clinicians in selecting appropriate radiopharmaceuticals for accurate imaging,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 340
retrieved_docs.content: CP 609754 is identified as having a narrower relationship to technetium Tc 99m arcitumomab, indicating that CP 609754 represents a more specific or limited form of this radiopharmaceutical. Technetium 99m compounds used for tumor detection are classified as members of both technetium Tc 99m arcitumomab and technetium Tc 99m depreotide, showing that these two agents belong to the broader category of technetium‑99m tumor‑detection radiopharmaceuticals. The radiopharmaceutical technetium Tc 99m arcitumomab is mapped from organotechneciové sloučeniny (organometallic compounds), reflecting its chemical origin in organometallic chemistry. In contrast, technetium Tc 99m depreotide is mapped from somatostatin, indicating that it is derived from the peptide hormone somatostatin. Together, these relationships clarify the hierarchical classification, specific variants, and molecular precursors of technetium‑99m agents used in nuclear imaging for tumor detection, which is essential for understanding their development, selection, and application in patient care.
